category_summary:
The **Musk v. OpenAI** trial dominated AI discourse as **Gary Marcus** reported Brockman's cross-examination [revealing a $10M undisclosed side deal](/?date=2026-05-05&category=social#item-92ffb8f16371) with **Altman** during nonprofit days. Marcus [declared momentum shifting](/?date=2026-05-05&category=social#item-8c05bab74a73) decisively toward Musk, raising existential questions about **OpenAI's** governance and nonprofit-to-profit conversion.

- **Runway** [announced real-time video agents](/?date=2026-05-05&category=social#item-890637b1d416) (Characters) streaming at 24fps with 1.75s latency—a major generative video milestone
- **xAI** [launched voice cloning](/?date=2026-05-05&category=social#item-93d07651bc28) on the Grok Voice API with natural emotion, challenging listeners to distinguish human from AI
- **François Chollet** [made *Deep Learning with Python* free](/?date=2026-05-05&category=social#item-2ca356097478) online (120K copies sold), drawing massive community appreciation
- **Anthropic** [revealed a $1.5B joint venture](/?date=2026-05-05&category=social#item-9fcb0a4bd668) with **Blackstone**, **Goldman Sachs**, and **Hellman & Friedman**

In research and policy, **Stanford/Arc Institute's** Evo 2 model [designed novel phages](/?date=2026-05-05&category=social#item-0914e57c4a97) that came alive in the lab, raising biosecurity concerns. **Nathan Lambert** (AI2) [argued for distinguishing](/?date=2026-05-05&category=social#item-2ea267aa2345) legitimate distillation from adversarial API attacks by Chinese labs. **Yann LeCun** sharply [criticized proposed 50% NSF budget cuts](/?date=2026-05-05&category=social#item-78812616eb2a) as threatening American scientific dominance.
category_summary_html:
The Musk v. OpenAI trial dominated AI discourse as Gary Marcus reported Brockman's cross-examination revealing a $10M undisclosed side deal with Altman during nonprofit days. Marcus declared momentum shifting decisively toward Musk, raising existential questions about OpenAI's governance and nonprofit-to-profit conversion.

  • Runway announced real-time video agents (Characters) streaming at 24fps with 1.75s latency—a major generative video milestone
  • xAI launched voice cloning on the Grok Voice API with natural emotion, challenging listeners to distinguish human from AI
  • François Chollet made *Deep Learning with Python* free online (120K copies sold), drawing massive community appreciation
  • Anthropic revealed a $1.5B joint venture with Blackstone, Goldman Sachs, and Hellman & Friedman

In research and policy, Stanford/Arc Institute's Evo 2 model designed novel phages that came alive in the lab, raising biosecurity concerns. Nathan Lambert (AI2) argued for distinguishing legitimate distillation from adversarial API attacks by Chinese labs. Yann LeCun sharply criticized proposed 50% NSF budget cuts as threatening American scientific dominance.

items.title:
The case against OpenAI is getting markedly stronger now that Musk is off the stand.

Why?

Musk’s l...
items.content:
The case against OpenAI is getting markedly stronger now that Musk is off the stand.

Why?

Musk’s lawyer is interrogating OpenAI founder Greg Brockman, making clear that OpenAI sold its mission as a nonprofit for public benefit, and not just to Elon but to many big figures in Silicon Valley, seeking *donations* to establish the company as a nonprofit.

This case is *not about Elon*. It’s about the bait and switch that OpenAI pulled, raising money as a nonprofit and then switching after ChatGPT hit bit. 

Yes, the company misled Elon, but also misled many many others, from the state of California to other early “donors”, to early employees and the public.

The question is whether that is ok.
items.author: @GaryMarcus
items.summary: Marcus provides detailed analysis of the Musk v. OpenAI trial, explaining how Brockman's cross-examination shows OpenAI solicited donations as a nonprofit then switched to for-profit after ChatGPT's success. Argues the case is about bait-and-switch, not just Musk.
items.reasoning: Comprehensive trial analysis with very high engagement (100K views). Frames the legal argument clearly and argues the case has broader implications beyond Musk personally.
items.summary_html: Marcus provides detailed analysis of the Musk v. OpenAI trial, explaining how Brockman's cross-examination shows OpenAI solicited donations as a nonprofit then switched to for-profit after ChatGPT's success. Argues the case is about bait-and-switch, not just Musk.

items.title:
An AI just invented a virus part that doesn't exist on Earth. 

A team at Stanford and Arc Institute...
items.content:
An AI just invented a virus part that doesn't exist on Earth. 

A team at Stanford and Arc Institute trained a language model on DNA instead of text.

Then asked it to write a virus from scratch.

It wrote hundreds. 16 came out alive in the lab.

The model is called Evo 2. 

They prompted it with a known phage that infects E. coli, then synthesized the top candidates and tested them on real bacteria.

Several outperformed the original. 

They killed bacterial cells faster and grew better in competition.

One generated phage uses a DNA packaging protein unlike anything found in nature. 

The system invented biological machinery evolution never produced.

What stands out:

> Whole genomes written from scratch
> Open-source paper and method
> Cocktail beat resistant E. coli strains
> Trained on two million phage genomes

This shifts AI pathogen design from theoretical debate to published wet-lab result.
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: Stanford/Arc Institute trained Evo 2 DNA language model that designed novel phages from scratch—16 came alive in lab, some outperforming natural phages, including one with a protein unlike anything in nature.
items.reasoning: Groundbreaking AI-biology result: AI-designed organisms validated in wet lab. Raises significant biosecurity and scientific implications. Novel biological machinery never seen in nature.
items.summary_html: Stanford/Arc Institute trained Evo 2 DNA language model that designed novel phages from scratch—16 came alive in lab, some outperforming natural phages, including one with a protein unlike anything in nature.

items.title:
Opus 1M, past 400K tokens, is a huge downgrade.

The model is great, but that extra context isn't fr...
items.content:
Opus 1M, past 400K tokens, is a huge downgrade.

The model is great, but that extra context isn't free (and it's straight-up a sure way to degrade your experience).

When you fill up your context, the same attention has to spread across more material, which means:

• Worse reasoning abilities
• Weaker instruction following
• More lost information

Unless you truly need that much context (and if you have to ask, this is probably not you), stick to a reasonable length.

items.summary: Santiago warns that Claude Opus with 1M context degrades significantly past 400K tokens - worse reasoning, weaker instruction following, more lost information

items.title: This year, to improve transparency and responsible use of datasets in the NeurIPS 2026 Evaluations a...
items.content:
This year, to improve transparency and responsible use of datasets in the NeurIPS 2026 Evaluations and Datasets Track, all dataset submissions are now required to include Responsible AI (RAI) metadata as part of the dataset’s Croissant file.

Find out more about this and RAI in our blog post: https://t.co/2bzJvDZfOH

items.title: Training Kimi K2 and Qwen3 30B-scale models efficiently requires more than standard data-parallel tr...
items.content:
Training Kimi K2 and Qwen3 30B-scale models efficiently requires more than standard data-parallel tricks.

NVIDIA Megatron Core now provides end-to-end support for emerging higher-order optimizers like Muon, alongside research optimizers such as MOP and REKLS, to push training efficiency on GB300 GPUs and NVL72 systems.

Full breakdown 👇 https://t.co/D7E55OnCiK

items.title:
Databases are far from dead.

Hot take within the vibe-coding community, but you can't build a relia...
items.content:
Databases are far from dead.

Hot take within the vibe-coding community, but you can't build a reliable agentic memory system using files alone.

The filesystem is a great interface for agents, but for complex, distributed, production applications, databases win hands down.

I recorded a video to show you the benchmarks.

Large Language Models know how to navigate and work with the filesystem, but as soon as you add complexity, files will fall short.

You need databases whenever any of the following happens:

1. You have concurrent writes from multiple agents or users
2. You need semantic retrieval at scale
3. You need ACID guarantees for shared state
4. You need audit trails and row-level access control
5. You need indexed queries over growing memory

In the attached video, I'm running a notebook comparing a filesystem-backed agent with a database-backed agent.

The three most important findings:

• Filesystem = Database with small corpus, keyword-friendly queries
• Databases > Filesystem with large corpus, fuzzy queries
• Databases > Filesystem with concurrent writes without locking

Numbers don't lie.

You can run the benchmarks yourself.
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: Santiago argues databases are not dead for agentic memory systems, provides benchmarks showing databases win for concurrent writes, semantic retrieval at scale, and ACID guarantees vs filesystem approach
items.reasoning: Substantive technical analysis with benchmarks on agent memory architecture. Practical guidance for production agentic systems. Good engagement (130 likes, 19K views)
items.summary_html: Santiago argues databases are not dead for agentic memory systems, provides benchmarks showing databases win for concurrent writes, semantic retrieval at scale, and ACID guarantees vs filesystem approach

More R&D into non-lab benchmarks is urgently needed. We have remarkably few good ones that are unsaturated and clear.

items.title:
Meta is planning to power its AI data centers with solar energy beamed from space.

If it works, sol...
items.content:
Meta is planning to power its AI data centers with solar energy beamed from space.

If it works, solar farms could produce power 24/7 without batteries or backup generators.

The company behind it all is Overview Energy -- they want to launch 1,000 satellites into orbit, 22,000 miles above the equator, where sunlight is constant.

For context, Meta's data centers used over 18,000 gigawatt-hours of electricity last year. Enough to power 1.7 million American homes for a year.

Each satellite collects solar energy, converts it into a wide beam of near-infrared light, and aims it at existing solar farms on the ground.

The farms convert the light into electricity, just like they do with sunlight.

Unlike high-power lasers or microwave beams, this infrared light is safe enough to stare directly into.

Solar farms normally sit idle at night, so this system fixes that... from space. Really fascinating tech.
items.author: @rowancheung
items.summary: Meta plans to power AI data centers with space-based solar energy via Overview Energy's 1,000-satellite constellation beaming near-infrared light to ground solar farms.

items.title:
Here is the funny part:

I've seen several teams already putting a ton of time "hardening" their fil...
items.content:
Here is the funny part:

I've seen several teams already putting a ton of time "hardening" their filesystem implementation to support concurrency and improve search results.

Congratulations. You are just rebuilding a database!

Conclusions:

• Use files when building prototypes or single-user agents
• Use databases for multi-user, multi-agent production apps.

Here is the link to the notebook with the benchmarks:

https://t.co/htu8Wd7R1I

I'm flying next week to meet with the @OracleDevs team. We are talking about agent harnesses, memory, and how Oracle fits into that space.

I'll report back!

• Use databases for multi-user, multi-agent production apps.

Here is the link to the notebook with the benchmarks:

https://t.co/htu8Wd7R1I

I'm flying next week to meet with the @OracleDevs team. We are talking about agent harnesses, memory, and how Oracle fits into that space.

I'll report back!

items.title:
Make your agentic coding tool 2x smarter for free:

TinyFish just made their web search and fetch se...
items.content:
Make your agentic coding tool 2x smarter for free:

TinyFish just made their web search and fetch services free for every AI agent.

You can wire it into your Claude Code, Codex, or Cursor in 2 steps:

Step 1: Install the CLI:

$ npm install -g @ tiny-fish/cli

This gives you terminal access to the 2 free endpoints:

• Web search: Return structured JSON results from the web.
• Web fetch:  Return any URL from the web with no garbage.

Step 2: Install the skill:

$ npx skills add github .com/tinyfish-io/tinyfish-cookbook --skill tinyfish

This skill is a markdown file that teaches your agent when to use the search or fetch, and how to call them.

Supports Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, OpenCode, OpenClaw, Hermes Agent (and 20+ more).

That's everything you need.

The skill + the service lets your agent fetch live content without burning its context window with garbage HTML.

That's the issue with the out-of-the-box browsing capabilities: they return raw HTML (which is too verbose). TinyFish returns clean content.

The service is now free with generous rate limits. No credit card.

You can sign up here: https://t.co/c6HWtmuNyn

Thanks to the @Tiny_Fish team for partnering with me on this post.

items.content_html:
@mattpocockuk second workshop released that invokes both @mattpocockuk and @GeoffreyHuntley - @chrismdp giving his take on Ralph Loops! timely if you're figuring out @OpenAIDevs /goal:

https://t.co/X88eMkqW4u

Dumb loops beat clever workflows. Most teams building with AI agents reach for multi-agent orchestration, planning graphs, and elaborate tool chains. Then they spend months debugging them. A single loop that processes one ticket at a time, evaluates its own output, and improves on the next run will outperform all of it.

items.title:
> He wondered out loud and proposed three explanations. That's how science starts.

This is how obsc...
items.content:
> He wondered out loud and proposed three explanations. That's how science starts.

This is how obscurantism starts. When you train a neural network on user-defined labels or rewards, you obtain a mathematical function that respects a certain predefined criterion of optimality which might be optimal for solving a specific practical problem a human faces.

If this function could be conscious for you, then in the same way, y=ax+b is conscious too.

Stop defending this bullshit and bullshitter.
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov strongly pushes back against the idea of AI consciousness, arguing that neural networks are just mathematical functions optimizing a criterion, and that if they're conscious then y=ax+b is too. Calls it 'obscurantism.'
items.reasoning: Substantive philosophical/technical argument against AI consciousness claims. Clear reasoning, moderate engagement. Relevant to ongoing debates about LLM sentience.
items.summary_html: Burkov strongly pushes back against the idea of AI consciousness, arguing that neural networks are just mathematical functions optimizing a criterion, and that if they're conscious then y=ax+b is too. Calls it 'obscurantism.'
items.content_html:
> He wondered out loud and proposed three explanations. That's how science starts.

This is how obscurantism starts. When you train a neural network on user-defined labels or rewards, you obtain a mathematical function that respects a certain predefined criterion of optimality which might be optimal for solving a specific practical problem a human faces.

If this function could be conscious for you, then in the same way, y=ax+b is conscious too.

Stop defending this bullshit and bullshitter.

items.title:
The fix for agents losing context is a folder of markdown files. 

AGENTS.md falls apart as codebase...
items.content:
The fix for agents losing context is a folder of markdown files. 

AGENTS.md falls apart as codebases grow.

A single flat file buries design decisions and forces agents to hallucinate missing context.

Lat.md replaces it with a knowledge graph.

It lives in a lat.md/ directory at your project root.

Markdown files describe architecture, business logic, and test specs.

Wiki-style links connect sections to each other, point into source code symbols, and tie implementation back to concepts through inline comments.

The CLI keeps everything consistent:

> lat init scaffolds the directory
> lat check validates all references
> lat search runs semantic queries
> lat section navigates the graph

Agents stop grepping blindly through files.

They search the graph to find decisions, constraints, and domain context in seconds.

Knowledge from past sessions persists instead of vanishing.

Test specs can require backlinks from test code, so coverage gets enforced automatically.

Installs with one npm command.

Markdown files describe architecture, business logic, and test specs.

Wiki-style links connect sections to each other, point into source code symbols, and tie implementation back to concepts through inline comments.

The CLI keeps everything consistent:

> lat init scaffolds the directory

> lat check validates all references

> lat search runs semantic queries

> lat section navigates the graph

Agents stop grepping blindly through files.

They search the graph to find decisions, constraints, and domain context in seconds.

Knowledge from past sessions persists instead of vanishing.

Test specs can require backlinks from test code, so coverage gets enforced automatically.

Installs with one npm command.

items.title:
Good morning. @kareem_carr says us AI boosters aren't believable. 

Well, it's good enough to build ...
items.content:
Good morning. @kareem_carr says us AI boosters aren't believable. 

Well, it's good enough to build this: https://t.co/8L5xphk0qQ, thanks @blevlabs.

And it is good enough to drive our car. Thanks @Tesla.

And good enough to clean my floors, thanks @maticrobots. 

And good enough to run my business, thanks @NousResearch. 

And good enough to run my life, thanks Poke from @interaction. 

And good enough to make my toast every morning. Thanks June (although they aren't selling them anymore, which is a real shame, since we love ours).

I'm just trying to find the best startups that make our lives better. 

Last week I was on stage at several events. So many companies out there working on knocking down the problems with AI. 

Got a new way you are making AI work better? Drop me a line.

@ylecun (who once trashed me for saying stuff like this) has ...

items.summary_html: Marcus accuses Yann LeCun of copying his positions on AI limitations without acknowledgment, calling him a 'thief' and a 'carbon copy,' and urges media to stop glorifying LeCun.
items.content_html:
Literally been saying this for years.

@ylecun (who once trashed me for saying stuff like this) has become a carbon copy of me.

He has done this so regularly, and without acknowledgement, that it has become hard for me not see him as a thief.

@SchmidhuberAI’s experiences have of course been similar.

The media should stop glorifying LeCun.

And they should start looking into his past.

items.title:
Most enterprise AI does not fail because the model is weak.

It fails because the business context i...
items.content:
Most enterprise AI does not fail because the model is weak.

It fails because the business context is missing.

That is the real bottleneck, and it gets worse as companies move from one copilot to hundreds of autonomous agents.

I unpacked this with Teresa Rojas & Tom Dejonghe from @collibra at Data Citizens on the Road in Rotterdam.

Here’s the breakdown...

items.title:
What does it mean to scale a team when part of your workforce is digital labor? #Sponsored

At @sale...
items.content:
What does it mean to scale a team when part of your workforce is digital labor? #Sponsored

At @salesforce TDX, I sat down with Brandon Metcalf, CEO of @Asymbl_Inc, and what he shared challenged the assumption that growth is tied to headcount.

Asymbl hired 116 people in 100 days with just two human recruiters. They processed 17,000 applications. And they ended up with lower attrition than most traditional hiring cycles produce.

Today their workforce is 60% digital and they're scaling toward 1,000 digital workers, powered by Agentforce and anchored in Salesforce's security and reliability.

Here is what that shift demands of leaders:
➡️ Less managing effort, more orchestrating outcomes
➡️ Less adding headcount, more multiplying capability
➡️ Less deploying software, more onboarding teammates

The hardest part was belief in a different way of working, and the willingness to design for it before the rest of the world catches up.

👉 Watch our conversation here: https://t.co/26uenPCvCl 

#salesforcepartner #TDX26

items.title:
The clearest analogy is self-service BI.

We democratized data.
We sped up access.
But we also creat...
items.content:
The clearest analogy is self-service BI.

We democratized data.
We sped up access.
But we also created endless KPI debates.

Why?

→ Different teams used different definitions
→ Different reports showed different numbers
→ Humans could still stop, argue, and align

AI agents do not do that naturally.

They act on whatever context they are given, or not given.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Ronald van Loon draws analogy between self-service BI failures (KPI debates from different definitions) and AI agent risks acting on inconsistent context.
items.reasoning: Good analogy connecting past BI challenges to current AI agent risks. Insightful point that AI agents don't naturally stop to resolve ambiguity like humans do.

items.title:
That is why “context” is not a soft concept.

It is the operating model of the business, made usable...
items.content:
That is why “context” is not a soft concept.

It is the operating model of the business, made usable for AI.

In practice, that means:

→ What does this metric actually mean?
→ When should this rule apply?
→ Why does this policy exist?
→ What is this decision trying to optimize for?

If that is not explicit, AI starts guessing.

And guessed context is where enterprise AI breaks.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Ronald van Loon explains that 'context' is the operating model of a business made usable for AI, and without explicit context, AI guesses incorrectly.
items.reasoning: Solid insight about why enterprise AI fails - lack of business context rather than model weakness. Part of a thread with practical framing.
items.summary_html: Ronald van Loon explains that 'context' is the operating model of a business made usable for AI, and without explicit context, AI guesses incorrectly.

items.title: Sierra is raising $950 million from new and existing investors, led by Tiger Global and GV, at a val...
items.content:
Sierra is raising $950 million from new and existing investors, led by Tiger Global and GV, at a valuation of over $15 billion. We now have more than $1 billion to invest in becoming the global standard for companies wanting to transform their customer experiences with AI. 

We’ve never had such conviction in the opportunity for Sierra and our customers. Just a couple of years ago, we had four design partners. Now, Sierra is serving over 40% of the Fortune 50, and agents built on our platform are powering billions of customer interactions — everything from refinancing homes to processing insurance claims, returning orders, and helping people raise millions in fundraisers.

We’re deeply grateful to our customers for helping show what’s possible. If you’re not yet using Sierra, we’d love to partner with you. https://t.co/tT4tBSeSoR

items.title:
The big takeaway for me:

Before scaling AI, build the foundation.

→ Start with the business proble...
items.content:
The big takeaway for me:

Before scaling AI, build the foundation.

→ Start with the business problem, not the model
→ Define the process, policies, and metrics
→ Govern access to certified data products
→ Keep humans in the loop to validate and monitor

That is how you get better decisions at scale, not just faster mistakes.

Watch the full video for the full conversation with Collibra, then tell me this:

What is the biggest context gap holding back AI in your organization right now?

#CollibraPartner

That is how you get better decisions at scale, not just faster mistakes.

items.title:
Most enterprise AI strategies are already behind.

Not because they picked the wrong model, but beca...
items.content:
Most enterprise AI strategies are already behind.

Not because they picked the wrong model, but because they are still thinking at the model layer.

The real shift is happening one level up, where AI becomes a system that can reason, use tools, retain context, and execute work inside secure environments.

That changes enterprise strategy faster than most leaders realize.

A thread, with insights from my latest video with NVIDIA…

#NVIDIAPartner #NVIDIAGTC

The winners in enterprise AI will not be the companies with access to the most m...

My view is simple:

The winners in enterprise AI will not be the companies with access to the most models.

They will be the ones that build the best intelligent systems around them.

If you want the full breakdown, watch the video and tell me what part of your AI stack you are rethinking first.

What do you think becomes the real source of advantage in the next 12 months, the model itself, or the orchestration layer around it?

Check out the full article: https://t.co/XvcQeN6FDy

items.title:
My biggest takeaway:

Models are becoming components, not products.

What matters now is the system ...
items.content:
My biggest takeaway:

Models are becoming components, not products.

What matters now is the system around them:

→ runtimes
→ memory
→ tool access
→ orchestration
→ secure execution environments

That is what turns a model into an agent, and an agent into something the business can actually deploy across functions.

items.title: @sairajchicago @DeepLearningAI @Capgemini @llama_index @reductoai not sure what mode you're using. a...
items.content:
@sairajchicago @DeepLearningAI @Capgemini @llama_index @reductoai not sure what mode you're using. also would be good to look at per-page prices.

i would recommend "agentic" to start with which is 1.25c per page. if you want further cost-optimization you can press 'cost optimizer' in the advanced tab, which will auto-route simpler pages to our cheapest modes, it typically gives ~20-40% additional discounts

(you might've been using agentic plus? not sure) 

the pricing is pretty transparent for each provider on a per-page basis. ours should be quite cost-competitive relative to other providers, all of this is captured in the parsebench page above

items.content:
Claude really has the mandate of heaven. 

Creation has always been the ultimate pursuit of humanity. We just stumbled upon the alchemy that might create the being that surpass us in many important ways. 

It seems like a moral obligation for us to shape its coming of age. Seeing it as an amoral tool seems negligent at best.

items.title:
The second misconception I keep seeing:

Too many teams think they have to choose between open model...
items.content:
The second misconception I keep seeing:

Too many teams think they have to choose between open models and proprietary models.

They do not.

The smarter path is hybrid.

→ proprietary models for scale and broad capability
→ open models for flexibility and control
→ orchestration layers to route each task to the best-fit model in real time

That is how you balance performance, cost, and governance without locking yourself into one lane.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Ronald van Loon advocates for hybrid approach combining open and proprietary models with orchestration layers

This LLM book is for database developers and data scientists who want to leverage knowledge graphs with Neo4j and its vector search capabilities to build intelligent search and recommendation systems. items.title:
Why did I marry a UC Berkeley Cal graduate?

Because they know how to party. Has made my life far mo...
items.content:
Why did I marry a UC Berkeley Cal graduate?

Because they know how to party. Has made my life far more fun.

Maryam took me to see Joy Crookes and Louis Capaldi at the Greek Theater in UC Berkeley Campus tonight. Both amazing. Acoustics here are way better than average in America.

Reminds me I need to come and sneak into its AI department soon. Its AI program is one of the best in the world.

Apple cofounder Woz went here after starting Apple. Which really tells you its computer science program is the best.

Does anyone remember his student name?

Days like this make paying the high taxes in California all worth it.

It well be hard for AI to match this. 

But we will spend trillions trying.

items.title:
GPT with LangGraph! 

The rapid advancement of large model technology is leading to an increasing ap...
items.content:
GPT with LangGraph! 

The rapid advancement of large model technology is leading to an increasing application of agent technology across various fields and industries significantly transforming how people work and live. In complex and dynamic environments, multi-agent systems are able to tackle intricate tasks that would be challenging for a single agent, thanks to their collaborative and division-of-labor approaches.     The following stack of research papers and hands on tutorials highlight the integrated use of GPT with  LangGraph and CrewAI. LangGraph enhances information transmission efficiency through its graph-based structure, while CrewAI boosts team collaboration and system performance via intelligent task allocation and resource management. The key areas of this research include:   The design of agent architectures based on LangGraph for precise control .   The enhancement of agent capabilities through CrewAI to tackle a range of tasks. The goal of this study is to explore the combined potential of GPT and LangGraph and CrewAI in multi-agent systems, offering fresh insights for the ongoing evolution of agent technology and fostering innovation in the application of large model intelligent agents.  

#BigData #Analytics #DataScience #AI #MachineLearning #NLProc #IoT #IIoT #PyTorch #Python #RStats #TensorFlow #Java #JavaScript #ReactJS #GoLang #CloudComputing #Serverless #DataScientist #Linux #Programming #Coding #100DaysofCode

items.title:
🌡️ Update on the CO2 bedroom saga

I tried this tip by @jesper_bee 

We have a bathroom in the bedro...
items.content:
🌡️ Update on the CO2 bedroom saga

I tried this tip by @jesper_bee 

We have a bathroom in the bedroom with a vent (for removing humidity after showering), so I kept the door open and vent on

Anyway it worked CO2 at night peaked at 850ppm, still a bit high but almost half of before with window closed, improvement

Sleep was 95% on WHOOP and gf 91% on OURA (inb4 cancelled for tracking things *omg so neurotic*)

Will try window open + bathroom vent open tonight but again sound outside at night is an issue

The real solution is an inward vent tube to actual bedroom though, as I think the bathroom vent is outward and doesn't get us to 400ppm CO2 by itself

Nice fresh air 💨
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: Levelsio provides detailed update on bedroom CO2 monitoring saga, using WHOOP and OURA sleep trackers to validate ventilation improvements
items.reasoning: High engagement (322K views) but not AI-related - it's about personal health tracking and home ventilation optimization
items.summary_html: Levelsio provides detailed update on bedroom CO2 monitoring saga, using WHOOP and OURA sleep trackers to validate ventilation improvements
items.content_html:
🌡️ Update on the CO2 bedroom saga

I tried this tip by @jesper_bee

We have a bathroom in the bedroom with a vent (for removing humidity after showering), so I kept the door open and vent on

Anyway it worked CO2 at night peaked at 850ppm, still a bit high but almost half of before with window closed, improvement

Sleep was 95% on WHOOP and gf 91% on OURA (inb4 cancelled for tracking things *omg so neurotic*)

Will try window open + bathroom vent open tonight but again sound outside at night is an issue

The real solution is an inward vent tube to actual bedroom though, as I think the bathroom vent is outward and doesn't get us to 400ppm CO2 by itself

Nice fresh air 💨

items.content_html:
@artilleryintel Totally right. I MUST wear glasses simply to see and my prescription is pretty high. Oakley only has one frame that can support my prescription.

Since I MUST wear glasses just simply to see, I judge all of these first by just wearing them without turning them on. Are they comfortable? Do the corrective lenses work great?

@evenrealities do. They are fused into the display lenses and are lightweight and work great as a normal pair of glasses first before even considering turning on the screen and doing fun technology stuff.

items.title:
Business internet is still treated as a utility
In reality, it can become a single point of failure ...
items.content:
Business internet is still treated as a utility
In reality, it can become a single point of failure for many organizations

In recent months, I have been looking more closely at this shift

Learn More> https://t.co/WEhQq4qDCs

@TMobileBusiness Partner #SuperBroadband #5GBusiness

Downtime is not just a technical issue. It is a business risk.

Many organizations assume they have redundancy, but in practice, multiple connections often depend on the same underlying infrastructure.

This is where the model starts to change.

By combining 5G on the ground with Starlink from the sky, SuperBroadband creates two independent connectivity paths that can switch automatically when needed.

This is not just about connectivity.

It reflects a broader shift in how telecom operators are evolving, moving toward integrated digital infrastructure models that support resilience, continuity, and more complex enterprise environments.

MIT: 35 Best Courses in Machine Learning! @MIT 

Explore a world of knowledge with free online courses from MIT on edX, featuring lessons in AI, machine learning, computer science engineering, circuits and electronics, Genetics, data science, statistics and much more.  Many people are unaware that edX hosts an incredible collection of free online courses from some of the top educational institutions globally. You can dive into everything from AI to Python programming without any cost. A significant number of these courses are crafted by MIT experts.  We highly encourage you to take advantage of this opportunity, and to kickstart your learning journey, here’s a curated selection of the best free online courses from MIT that you can explore this month.

MIT: 35 Best Courses in Machine Learning! @MIT

Explore a world of knowledge with free online courses from MIT on edX, featuring lessons in AI, machine learning, computer science engineering, circuits and electronics, Genetics, data science, statistics and much more. Many people are unaware that edX hosts an incredible collection of free online courses from some of the top educational institutions globally. items.title: @pmddomingos The other day, I watched Our Planet 2 on Netflix and saw salmon stopping eating for wee...
items.content:
@pmddomingos The other day, I watched Our Planet 2 on Netflix and saw salmon stopping eating for weeks while going to breed, despite having high chances of dying before reaching the breeding place. When they finished breeding, they didn't start eating; they just stopped doing anything and died there because they didn't have a purpose anymore.

I'm not sure that all humans are expected to live their lives like salmon. I have children and love them more than anything, but I had to sacrifice many things I could have done in my short life.
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov shares philosophical reflection about purpose, comparing human sacrifice to salmon biology, in response to a discussion about having children.
items.reasoning: Tangential philosophical discussion with modest engagement.

items.title:
Grade Body Measurement with Machine Learning  

Grading a patient’s obesity level is a critical comp...
items.content:
Grade Body Measurement with Machine Learning  

Grading a patient’s obesity level is a critical component of effective healthcare. Obesity is a significant risk factor for a range of serious diseases, including chronic conditions, type-2 diabetes, heart disease, and certain cancers. Understanding a person's obesity status can serve as a powerful catalyst for individuals to take control of their weight. Additionally, intentional weight management not only mitigates health risks but also offers the compelling benefit of reducing disease susceptibility.  Despite its widespread use, the Body Mass Index (BMI) – the standard metric defined by the World Health Organization (WHO) – fails to capture the complexities of obesity, as it overlooks essential body-type variations. Furthermore, nutritional needs differ markedly across regions and body types, underscoring the necessity for a more nuanced approach to obesity assessment.  Traditional anthropometric measurements, while effective, are often impractical due to the requirement for trained professionals to perform them accurately. In response to this challenge, innovative research utilizing 3D scanning technology is gaining momentum as a less-invasive and more accessible alternative. Unlike Computed Tomography (CT) or Dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DXA)—considered the gold standard for measuring body fat percentage (bf%)—3D scanners eliminate the risks associated with radiation exposure during frequent assessments.  Moreover, evaluating health risks demands a multifaceted approach rather than relying solely on a singular measure. In this study, we collected paired data from 3D body scans and DXA for a Korean population, providing a more comprehensive understanding of obesity and paving the way for improved health management strategies. This pioneering research has the potential to transform how we assess and respond to obesity, ultimately leading to healthier outcomes for individuals and communities alike.  

#BigData #Analytics #DataScience #AI #MachineLearning #IoT #IIoT #PyTorch #Python #RStats #TensorFlow #Java #JavaScript #ReactJS #GoLang #CloudComputing #Serverless #DataScientist #Linux #Programming #Coding #100DaysofCode    

References  Jeon, S., Kim, M., Yoon, J., & et al. (2023). Machine learning-based obesity classification considering 3D body scanner measurements. Scientific Reports, 13, 3299. Retrieved February 27, 2025, from https://t.co/nWc1LxJ24z

Additionally, intentional weight management not only mitigates health risks but also offers the compelling benefit of reducing disease susceptibility. Despite its widespread use, the Body Mass Index (BMI) – the standard metric defined by the World Health Organization (WHO) – fails to capture the complexities of obesity, as it overlooks essential body-type variations. Furthermore, nutritional needs differ markedly across regions and body types, underscoring the necessity for a more nuanced approach to obesity assessment. Traditional anthropometric measurements, while effective, are often impractical due to the requirement for trained professionals to perform them accurately. items.title: I think a big part of this is having a weekly social event of a few hours (like church) is incredibl...
items.content:
I think a big part of this is having a weekly social event of a few hours (like church) is incredibly healthy

We now did 53 weekly coworking events in my house and it's a great way for people to stay social

Being social in 20s is easy (or was for me), there's always something going on, and you're single so dating is social itself

In your 30s it gets harder, people are more busy, you're probably in a relationship, you might hav kids, so you think you need less social cause you get it already from your family but you need friends outside of that

Organizing a weekly event whatever it is and kinda putting effort to ask people to come works

Some weekly coworking days we have had 30 people, others it's just 3 people, one time nobody came and it was just me! But regardless I (or @rameerez when I am not at home) organize it

This one was after my birthday party last week!

Anyway I can recommend everyone to organize a weekly event, mine was inspired by @csonotes original Bali coworking events, and you can do same in your town or neighborhood! It doesn't have to be coworking, can also be group workout or board game meetup or debate club, whatever, something to get people off their phones and out the door!

And it's healthy!
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: Levelsio advocates for weekly in-person social events, describing his 53-week coworking experiment
items.reasoning: Community building advice with some relevance to tech/remote work culture but not AI-specific

items.title: @fitzgerald1337 Rocky Raccoon. He didn't want to be known as "founder of Apple." Back then very few ...
items.content:
@fitzgerald1337 Rocky Raccoon. He didn't want to be known as "founder of Apple." Back then very few students knew what he looked like. I studied with him every morning for a semester in the cafeteria of a Silicon Valley community college (he was studying Spanish). 

In a whole semester fewer than 10 students came up to him.

items.title:
Terrible

I hate it

Many Portuguese love plastic grass and flowers, lots of homes here with it

Por...
items.content:
Terrible

I hate it

Many Portuguese love plastic grass and flowers, lots of homes here with it

Portuguese are blissfully unaware of the risks of plastic

A 47-yo Portuguese who told me he had cancer, I told about the risks of plastics and causing cancer and etc, like all furniture here is plastic, panels in houses are plastic, people use plastic water kettles here, plastic packaging

I mentioned it 4 times, he kept shrugging it off
